title: ‘Spiderhead’ and the Five Netflix Movies That Matter
format: Sean and Chris Ryan revisit a 2021 movie auction, review Joseph Kosinski's 'Spiderhead,' and take stock of Netflix's original-movie era. Then, Rhett Reese and Paul Wernick on adapting George Saunders and ten years of false starts.

segments.heading: The 2021 movie auction, revisited
segments.note: Chris Ryan's picks, one by one

picks.title: Spiderhead
picks.note: Bought sight-unseen for $80 as 'Escape From Spider-Head' — the reason for this episode.

picks.title: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
picks.note: $50, and Sean is fairly sure Chris still hasn't seen it.

picks.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
picks.note: $230 — called it a bargain, given it was the movie sensation of late 2021.

picks.title: Red, White and Water
picks.note: Roughly $490 — and it still doesn't have a release date, so the auction remains technically unresolved.

segments.heading: 'Spiderhead'
segments.note: an adaptation of George Saunders's short story

films.title: Spiderhead
films.note: Joseph Kosinski directs Miles Teller and Jurnee Smollett as convicts volunteering for emotion-altering drug trials run by Chris Hemsworth's prison overseer. Both call it a solid, well-crafted 'B-plus movie' — Sean compares its themes of soft workplace control to Severance, Chris flags a soft-rock-over-harrowing-imagery trick that goes back to Reservoir Dogs, and both think Hemsworth, playing a motor-mouthed tech villain, is doing the real heavy lifting.

segments.heading: Is Netflix's original-movie business in trouble?
segments.note: the 'reliable middle' and the quality problem

films.title: Bright
films.note: The moment things really soured, per Chris — 'everything about this is off.'

films.title: The Cloverfield Paradox
films.note: Dropped right after the Super Bowl; the other marker of the shift into buying up studio blockbusters-in-waiting.

films.title: Bird Box
films.note: Hundreds of millions watched it and it became a meme, but neither host thinks anyone would call it good.

films.title: Red Notice
films.note: Sean's guess: of the 350 million who watched it, fewer than 50 million actually thought it was good.

films.title: 6 Underground
films.note: Both hosts genuinely like it — critically reviled, but a self-aware Michael Bay pastiche that works for them.

films.title: Extraction
films.note: A solid Chris Hemsworth action movie, if not exactly rewatchables material.

segments.heading: The good stuff Netflix doesn't get enough credit for
segments.note: a three-month run in 2017 they can't seem to repeat

films.title: Triple Frontier
films.note: A years-in-development-hell project they actually got made — "we love Triple Frontier here."

films.title: The Lost Daughter
films.note: A literary adaptation from a first-time filmmaker (Maggie Gyllenhaal) that Chris calls terrific.

films.title: Okja
films.note: Netflix was early on Bong Joon Ho — bittersweet in hindsight once he won the Oscar for Parasite instead.

films.title: White Noise
films.note: Noah Baumbach's $140 million adaptation — flagged as possibly the last of these giant vanity-project checks Netflix writes.

films.title: I Don't Feel at Home in This World Anymore
films.note: Part of the 2017 stretch: two Macon Blair movies, a Charlie McDowell film, and Win It All inside three months.

films.title: Small Crimes
films.note: Macon Blair wrote it.

films.title: The Discovery
films.note: Charlie McDowell's vaguely sci-fi entry from that same 2017 run.

films.title: Win It All
films.note: A movie both hosts love.

films.title: The Gentlemen
films.note: Still floating in and out of Netflix's top 10 years later — Chris thinks people mistake it for a new Guy Ritchie movie made for the service.

films.title: El Camino: A Breaking Bad Movie
films.note: Cited as a model for reviving old IP as a single movie instead of a new season.

films.title: Blonde
films.note: Andrew Dominik's next, due out in a few months — both hosts are genuinely excited.

segments.heading: In conversation
segments.guest: Rhett Reese and Paul Wernick on
segments.note: adapting George Saunders, and ten years of stops and starts

films.title: Spiderhead
films.note: Optioned from Condé Nast in June 2012; the pair originally wrote it on spec intending to direct it themselves before Deadpool took over their lives. Kosinski eventually came aboard and sold it to Netflix.

films.title: Deadpool
films.note: Took six years to get made; they cite the freeway ambush scene as an example of how precisely they script action, down to each bullet.

films.title: Deadpool 2
films.note: Home of the Davis Love III joke Reese kept fighting to keep in the script.

films.title: Zombieland
films.note: Their first collaboration; led to being pitched every zombie/vampire/werewolf script in town afterward.

films.title: Zombieland: Double Tap
films.note: Took ten years to get made.

films.title: G.I. Joe: Retaliation
films.note: The Himalayas rope sequence, written with zero dialogue, was their most fun action sequence to write.

I Don't Feel at Home in This World Anymo.title: I Don't Feel at Home in This World Anymore

El Camino: A Breaking Bad Movie.title: El Camino: A Breaking Bad Movie
